result:
The rain tapped against the windowpane with the erratic rhythm of a dying heartbeat. The room was drowning in the gray light of a Tuesday afternoon. There was nothing remarkable about this day, nothing to suggest that the fabric of reality was about to fray. I watched a single bead of water trace a jagged path down the glass, gathering smaller drops until it became heavy enough to plummet from sight. The silence in the room was heavy, almost oppressive. I stood up, the floorboards groaning in protest, and walked toward the hallway. At the end of the hall stood the closet door. It had always been just a closet, holding winter coats and forgotten umbrellas. But today, a faint, unnatural luminescence bled from beneath the threshold. It wasn't the warm yellow of a hallway lamp, nor the sterile white of daylight. It was a shifting, iridescent glow, like the colors of an oil slick on wet pavement. I reached for the brass knob. It was ice-cold. I turned it, and stepped through.

I did not step onto the scuffed linoleum of a closet. My foot crunched onto a surface that sounded like shattered crystal. I blinked against a sudden, blinding brilliance. I was standing in a forest, but not one grown from soil and seed. The trees were towering structures of spun glass, their trunks twisting and spiraling upward into a canopy of crystalline leaves. The light here came from everywhere and nowhere, refracting through millions of prismatic surfaces to cast rainbows across the ground. The ground itself was a mosaic of jagged glass fragments. I took a step, and the forest responded. A breeze, carrying the scent of ozone and crushed mint, swept through the glass branches. It produced a melody, a cacophony of delicate chimes, ringing and echoing in a chaotic yet perfectly harmonious symphony. It was beautiful, but it was a cold beauty. I walked deeper into the glass woods. My reflection shattered and reformed in the trunks as I passed. The forest seemed to capture not just light, but time and potential. I reached out to touch a low-hanging leaf. It was perfectly smooth, colder than the doorknob had been, and vibrated with a low, humming frequency that traveled up my arm and settled in my chest. I realized then that the forest was not dead; it was merely alive in a way I could not fully comprehend. The ringing intensified, a crescendo of crystalline voices urging me forward.

The glass forest gave way abruptly. One moment I was surrounded by dazzling light, and the next, I stood on the edge of a precipice overlooking a vast, rolling sea. But there was no water here. It was an ocean of pale gray ash. The sky above was a heavy, bruised purple, devoid of sun, moon, or stars. The ash moved like water, swelling into slow, majestic waves that crested and broke without a sound. A profound, crushing silence replaced the chiming of the forest. I felt a deep ache in my bones, a sorrow that did not belong to me. I descended the slope to the shoreline. The edge of the ash sea lapped at my toes.
